topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope sat on the worn kitchen table, a stark white rectangle mocking me. My hands were shaking as I picked it up, the paper crinkling under my grip like dry leaves. Inside, the letter was brief, clinical, announcing a relocation to Seattle. Seattle. A whole ocean away. I reread the words three times, each sentence landing like a small, sharp stone in my chest. I didn't look up when Liam walked into the room, whistling tunelessly. "Hey, what's got you looking so glum?" His easy smile didn't reach his eyes, and the casualness of his tone felt like a deliberate insult. I slammed the letter down, the sound echoing in the sudden silence. "Nothing," I managed, my voice tight, too high. I stood up abruptly, needing distance, needing to put a wall between us before I did something I couldn't take back.

[story 2]

I watched Liam pack his boxes with a detached fascination, the sheer volume of his belongings a physical manifestation of the distance growing between us. Every corner he turned, every heavy cardboard box he wrestled into the moving truck, sent a jolt of unpleasantness through me. It wasn't sadness, not exactly; it was a deep, churning resentment that settled low in my stomach. When he finally looked over, his expression was one of breezy optimism, the kind reserved for someone about to embark on an exciting adventure. "Ready for the big leap?" he asked, clapping me on the shoulder. I flinched, pulling away subtly. "Yeah," I mumbled, focusing intently on a smudge on the floor, trying to keep my breathing even. The air in the room felt thick, charged with unspoken accusations that hung heavy between the unspoken agreement of our friendship.

[story 3]

The news hit me during a late-night coffee run. The barista’s cheerful greeting seemed jarring against the sudden, cold realization that Liam was planning to vanish. I gripped my mug so tightly my knuckles turned white, the ceramic digging painfully into my palms. I didn't say anything to him when he casually mentioned his new apartment plans. I just nodded, a stiff, jerky motion, and focused on the steam rising from my drink, trying to analyze the pattern of the swirling vapor as if it held some hidden clue to the betrayal. My jaw felt locked, a rigid set that refused to relax. When he offered a reassuring squeeze of my arm, I subtly shifted my weight away, creating a small, deliberate space between us, a silent declaration that this change felt like a deliberate severing.
